summary.intent: Integrate cargo-crap (CRAP metric gate) into xtask CI tooling and improve CI speed by removing redundant steps and adding apt caching.
summary.outcome: Implemented cargo-crap in baseline/regression mode (--fail-regression --min 30), removed redundant check job, rewired CI fan-out to remove a compile stage from the critical path, added native-fast coverage to xci-fast gate, and added apt caching for LLVM-22 and dioxus desktop deps via composite action. At session end the second xci-fast validation run was still in progress and the commit/push had not yet been completed.

repo: CLAUDE.md is a symlink to AGENTS.md, which is the canonical file also referenced by mutants.rs.
spec/testing/14-ci.md is the authoritative CI spec and must be kept in sync with any ci.yml changes.
ars-derive is a proc-macro intentionally unmeasured and ars-e2e is not coverage-gated; both require --allow under cargo-crap --workspace rather than --exclude.
cargo-crap --exclude is a silent no-op under --workspace; --allow must be used instead (verified empirically: ars-derive showed 47 entries with --exclude, 0 with --allow).

State-machine components (Machine::transition) intrinsically have CC 30-287 and are fully tested but structurally high-complexity; 34 library functions exceed CRAP threshold 30 at 100% coverage because CRAP == CC at 100%.
check_all in coverage.rs treats packages with lines_found==0 as SKIP not FAIL, so a native-only lcov passed to the full threshold list silently skips wasm-only crates rather than failing.
CI failures had two distinct root causes: (A) flaky ars-dom instrumented wasm browser test aborting coverage runs early, and (B) a legitimate threshold regression on the PR.
Native coverage matches CI numbers for non-wasm crates (ars-components, ars-core, etc.), making native-only checks faithful for those crates specifically.

code.finding: run_coverage produces a merged lcov.info at the repo root; cargo-crap can reuse it directly with zero recompile cost.

clippy 1.96 (released 2026-05-25) introduced a deny-by-default unnecessary_option_map_or_else lint; fix is to replace map_or_else(|| clamp_page(...), identity) with unwrap_or_else(|| clamp_page(...)).
native_thresholds() must be derived from default_thresholds() minus wasm coverage targets minus ars-derive to prevent drift between the two threshold lists.

workflow: cargo-crap --fail-regression alone is too noisy: it fires on any CRAP increase above epsilon even for sub-threshold functions (CC 3->5 triggers it). Pairing with --min 30 limits alerts to above-threshold regressions only.
--fail-regression treats new functions as 'new' not 'regressed' (exit 0), which scales naturally for state-machine repos that continuously add components.
Absolute CRAP threshold 30 conflicts with state-machine architectures where CRAP == CC at 100% coverage; baseline/regression mode is the correct gate for mature codebases.
GHA-only changes (apt caches, CRAP install, fan-out rewiring) cannot be validated locally and should be marked 'validates on first CI run' rather than claiming local verification.

Apt package caching via actions/cache on /var/cache/apt/archives is low-risk and additive: cache miss behaves exactly as before, cache hit seeds the apt archive. Baking it into a composite action gives all consumer jobs the benefit at once.

Background bash task exit codes can be masked when using pipes: echo after a pipe captures tail's exit code, not cargo's. Must capture ${PIPESTATUS[0]} before piping or avoid backgrounding commands with pipes.

friction: cargo-crap --exclude silently no-ops under --workspace, discovered only after empirical testing; would have shipped a broken gate without this discovery.
Stale lcov.info (one month old) initially reported 481/9064 over-threshold functions while a fresh run showed 200, causing misleading calibration that required an extra full coverage run to resolve.
Background command exit code masking made xci-fast appear to pass (exit 0 reported) when it had actually failed at the clippy stage, requiring a second full ~15 minute gate run.
clippy 1.96 landed mid-session (2026-05-25) and immediately broke the workspace with a new deny-by-default lint on pre-existing unrelated code, requiring an unplanned fix before the PR could pass.
Build lock contention: a background instrumented coverage run locked cargo, causing foreground validation attempts to stall.
An interactive cp alias blocked a direct file copy during fresh-vs-fresh gate validation.
open_items: Branch protection: a repo admin must drop the 'Workspace Check' required status check after this PR merges or future PRs will hang indefinitely.
Flaky ars-dom instrumented wasm browser test (Failure A) needs dedicated triage and quarantine rather than retry workarounds; deferred as a separate issue.
.crap-baseline.json is 2.4 MB (full baseline); flooring with --min 15 would shrink diffs ~10x at the cost of missing single-PR jumps from far-below to >=30 -- explicitly deferred.
Pre-existing stale ASCII job dependency graph in spec/testing/14-ci.md section 5 uses node names (e.g. core-tests) that never matched real job names; flagged but not fixed to avoid scope creep.
kache evaluation: needs a benchmark branch with cold/warm CI wall-clock numbers before an adoption decision can be made; deferred.
Custom CI image evaluation: justified only if CI setup time becomes sustained multi-job pain; deferred.
PR->native-only / nightly->full coverage split: a larger coverage speedup option that trades feedback latency for runner minutes; deferred.
Second xci-fast run was still executing at transcript end; commit and push to the PR branch had not yet been completed.
